text: Hello everybody! I have big news. I applied to SNU and was accepted.
Woo! Just kidding. Today, I am taking a tour of the campus to teach you university English. Let's get started.
I came across some people playing soccer at the soccer field. It makes me think of football in the US
because college sports are huge in America. I have so many good memories
of going to the college football stadium and watching a game. My tour guide just arrived.
Hi Sylvia! This is Sylvia from Finland and she's a full-time student here at SNU. What are you majoring in?
Korean linguistic. I think she's Korean at this point. She's totally fluent. What's your favorite place on campus?
Maybe the pond or the library. I always love a university library. So what's our first stop on today's tour?
I think we should go that way. Then we will pass like college of art and then college of shimany. Let's go!
So you did your bachelor's in Finland? Yes, in Helsinki. How is it different? In Finland, it's more individualistic.
We don't have this one campus where everything is located. Also, the professor, student, relationship is different.
In Korea, I feel like they tend to help more or they are more interested even in personal level how we are doing.
Or maybe that's also because I'm a foreigner. I'm very lucky to have these kinders. I love how this campus is surrounded by nature.
It's very peaceful. The students must need this. They have such a stressful life.
Yeah, doing homework and studying all the time. It must be nice to walk outside and enjoy this nature.
This is the new library behind me. I love visiting university libraries.
Even though it's summer, I bet there are lots of students inside cramming for tests or pulling all nighters.
I heard that you can get a meal at the cafeteria for chonon. So I'm going to head there now and grab a bite to eat.
Let's find somewhere to sit. Wow, I can't believe this is only chonon. That's less than one US dollar.
So cheap. And it actually looks quite nice. You've got rice, some radish, and side dishes. And this, a fried piece. Let's see.
I think it's chicken. It's similar to tang suyuk. Not bad. In the US, it's quite different.
Most students have a meal plan that gives them access to different cafeterias on campus. And they pay that as part of their tuition.
Is that shade? Let's go return my tray.
You can buy lots of SNU merch at the store here. You can tell they have a lot of school spirit.
In Korea, I notice a lot of university students wearing varsity jackets. I don't think it's quite as common in the US.
Most people in the US just buy hoodies or sweatpants.
I found the school mascot. It's so cute.
I feel like I'm hiking now. Since Korea is so mountainous, I realized a lot of universities are all uphill.
You have to work hard to get to classes.
We picked up another friend on our campus tour. Can you introduce yourself?
Of course. My name is Hang Ji, and I am now graduated from Seoul National University. Thank you.
I studied business and computer science, and I'm actually from the States, so I lived there all my life.
And then I came here for college, spent five years here.
How do you think college life here in Korea compares to college life in the US?
Maybe a clear similarity is that you start off with your gen ed classes.
So everyone takes the same classes in their freshman year as in the States.
And then you move into your major specific courses in your second, third, and fourth years.
But then a difference, I would say, is that you have more time in college in Korea to think about what you want to do.
So most of the time, freshman year is just spent partying.
You spend 18 years of your life studying, so now you're off to school and you're partying.
And then sophomore year, it's like a transition period.
And then I would say junior and senior year is really when you start to think about what you want to do.
And in these years, I've noticed a lot of students will take a gap year just to take a breather or to think about what they want to do or pursue an internship.
Whereas in the States, I've noticed that people graduate in four years.
The gap years aren't very common and people will start to do internships very early on.
Even your first or second year, do people take electives as well?
Yeah, of course.
So electives are required in some sense.
It's kind of contradicting.
But because you have to fulfill a certain number of credits.
Some fun electives that I've done have been sports ones.
So there's golf and there's pilates.
I've also taken a class about Disney, so that was really fun too.
So electives are definitely part of our curriculum here as well.
Another really big difference is the club culture.
So you make a lot of friends doing clubs here.
Whereas in the States, I don't know if club culture is nearly as big of a part of college life.
In the US, Greek life is a big part of the college experience.
But you don't have it here in Korea, right?
No, not at all.
In American colleges, we have something called Greek life.
Girls can go to sororities and boys can be in fraternities.
And you pay money to be in this club.
They become kind of your college family.
They even call each other sisters or brothers.
So at my college, there was one street and it was filled with sororities and fraternities.
And it was known as the party street.
They're kind of known for being party animals.
Sometimes there is a problem with hazing, which is bullying the first year.
Not always the case, but it happens.
In the US, you were required to live on campus for the first, at least semester.
I want to say the first year, even, maybe.
My first year of college, I went in blind.
I didn't know my roommates at all.
I had three roommates and we say go in blind when you don't know who they are.
They're just randomly assigned to you.
I got really lucky. I had great roommates.
When I lived in a dorm, we had an RA.
And basically, they were the babysitter or the boss of your floor.
They just had to make sure that you didn't have alcohol in your room.
My RA was really cool.
So he'd always give us a warning.
He'd knock on the door and say, if you have anything to hide, hide it now.
I think they have a similar system here in Korea.
Are the dorms separated by gender?
Or are any of the dorms coed?
Undergrad dorms are separated.
Promostors, students.
There's one building where both females and males live, but they have different floors.
This is one of the top schools.
So I feel like you must get a lot of homework.
It must be really tough here.
Can you tell me a little bit about that?
Like I said, I major in business and computer science.
So I think the majors are very different.
In business, I don't really get that many homework assignments.
Rather, it's like one big term project or like a group project in CS, computer science.
We get a lot of homework.
So it's pretty heavy on the amount of course work we have to do.
How about you?
During masters, instead of homework, we have one bigger final report that we submit.
So in order to graduate, since you're a master's student, do you have to write a thesis?
Yes, I'm working on my thesis at the moment.
I have been working on it for a few semesters now, and I will graduate next semester.
Congratulations!
You guys seem like very hard workers.
I remember skipping class a lot in college.
Do you ever skip?
We have a system where you can just tap your ID card on this attendance checker.
It's all electronic.
Or you can even do a QR scan of your student ID.
A lot of people will just tap or scan, then they will leave really fast before the professor walks in.
But when it's like 300 people in one class, it's hard for the professor to know who's there or not.
For us, our class size is very small, usually less than 10 people.
And professors are close with a master's student.
So even if they don't look at the name list, they remember who's in the class.
So it's harder in your classes.
You can't get away with much.
I hear the terms sunbae and hubei in Korea here.
Is that real?
It is definitely real.
We definitely do have a sunbae hubei culture.
So one of the ways that the sunbaes will help the hubeis
is that they will pass down what's called chokpo,
which is basically a collection of previous tests and homework assignments that this professor has given.
And that is one of the ways that you can be more prepared for a class
than maybe your peers who don't have that sunbae hubei relationship.
Also, it's just the nature of Korea.
It's very hierarchical.
So you have like, onni-upal.
So that makes it really easy to maintain a sunbae hubei relationship.
And it's a really easy way to call them if you forget their name.
That's the cheat code.
I'm jealous.
I want sunbae.
You just graduated.
You're about to graduate.
What are your next plans in life?
I am actually off to law school in the States.
I will be continuing my studies for the next three years.
After law school, I'm still undetermined on what exactly I want to pursue.
But I'm really looking forward to continuing to study and getting started on my legal career.
Good luck.
Thank you.
That's amazing. Good luck.
Thank you.
I want to do my PhD here in Korea.
Will you get your PhD at SNU?
Yes, that's what I want to do.
Congratulations.
Well, thank you ladies so much for joining me today and giving me a tour of the school.
It made me reminisce, so I appreciate it.
Of course, thank you for having us on your channel.
Anytime, anytime.
Thank you.
Thanks for watching, and I'll see you next time.
Bye.
